about_spirit_intro: The TCP/UP project was born out of a simple need: to restore clarity to creative work at a time when hybrid content is becoming the norm.
about_spirit_nostalgie_text: I belong to a generation that grew up with Goldorak and discovered the emergence of the future while listening to Iron Maiden on a cassette Walkman. I never really stopped being that guy who takes everything apart to understand how it works. My journey began with games on the Atari VCS 2600, before I discovered the creative power of the Atari ST. I experienced the world before algorithms, in the early, tentative days of the Internet. That geeky, hands-on, deeply curious culture has never left me.
about_spirit_libre_title: An identity rooted in free and open-source software
about_spirit_libre_intro: My commitment to transparency is nothing new. As early as the 2000s, I was actively involved in the free software blogosphere, notably within the Ubuntu-fr community. Under the pseudonym "https://www.freetux.net/taltan-pour-les-mouches/"Taltan ("T'as le temps" / "You've got time"), I kept a technical logbook whose goal was already to make computing accessible, free, and shared.
about_spirit_libre_mission: At the time, I was writing educational procedures to help users appropriate GNU/Linux. Today, with TCP/UP, I am continuing that same mission: giving users the keys to understanding technologies that, without transparency, risk becoming black boxes.
about_spirit_libre_philosophy: I am a strong advocate of the philosophy of free software and sharing. To me, a digital tool is only worthwhile through what it enables people to build together, in trust. This conviction naturally led me to take an interest in the challenges of artificial intelligence, not as a gadget, but as a lever that must be framed with clarity and honesty.
about_spirit_expertise_title: Expertise in the service of the user
about_spirit_expertise_intro: Professionally, I have spent more than 20 years working behind the scenes of digital systems within the State. I devoted most of my career to the evolution of critical information systems, often through the lens of Project Ownership Assistance (AMOA).
about_spirit_expertise_role: My role has always consisted in placing user needs back at the center of the machine, serving as a bridge between business usage and technical complexity. Leading infrastructure and security projects in demanding environments taught me one fundamental thing: trust is not decreed, it is built through rigor and transparency. My commitment to public service defines my approach: digital technology must remain a tool in the service of humans, and not the other way around.
about_spirit_why_text: Today, Artificial Intelligence is transforming our lives at an unprecedented speed. But in this acceleration, we risk losing sight of what matters most: who does what? Faithful to my roots of sharing and transparency, I created TCP/UP to give power back to users. This is not about rejecting AI (it is a fantastic creative partner), but about assuming its use with the same honesty as open-source code. It seemed useful to me to propose a simple language of transparency, capable of helping distinguish what comes from human intention, AI assistance, and co-construction.
about_ia_heading: How I used AI to create TCP/UP (and why I stand by it)
about_ia_quote: “I could not defend transparency without subjecting myself to it.”
about_ia_intro1: The TCP/UP protocol is not only a standard I propose; it is a tool I embody. It seemed incoherent (even contradictory) to design a transparency-driven approach without applying it with total honesty to the very genesis of this project.
about_ia_intro2: This site and its documentation are the result of a clear-eyed co-creation between human intelligence and artificial intelligence. Below is a detailed account of this collaboration, segmented according to the pillars of the protocol.
about_ia_resume_list:
• Concept, Philosophy & Rules: [TCP/UP: HCE] (Human-origin foundation, assisted form)
  • Web Development & Code: [TCP/UP: ACE] (Co-construction)
  • Visual resources: Composite nature (Mix of manual creation and generation)
  • Final arbitration: full human responsibility. Final choices rest with the author.
about_ia_hce_intro: All of the project’s content, its philosophy, its matrix structure, the distinction between Content and Form, as well as the definition of the 5 labels, are the result of my own reflection.
about_ia_hce_list_item1: A formulation and structuring tool: able to refine, organize, and put into words concepts I had previously defined
about_ia_hce_list_item2: A sparring partner: used to challenge the robustness of the protocol, test the consistency of arbitration rules, and simulate critical cases
about_ia_hce_conclusion: This is the essence of the HCE (Human-Centric Editing) label: the intention and intellectual architecture are mine; the AI served as a tool for formulation, structuring, and stress-testing.
about_ia_ace_intro: The development of the site and its interface followed a different, more hybrid logic.
about_ia_ace_desc: While I defined the architecture, user needs, and ergonomic choices, the production of the code (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) involved a continuous back-and-forth. The AI acted as a technical co-pilot, generating complex code blocks that I then audited, corrected, and assembled.
about_ia_ace_label: Here, the AI actively participated in the production of technical material. The ACE (AI-Centric Editing) label therefore naturally applies: a close fusion where the tool produces, but the human directs.
about_ia_visual_intro: Like any modern digital project, TCP/UP is a composite object.
about_ia_visual_item1: The logo and visual identity: the result of manual creation and personal aesthetic choices.
about_ia_visual_item2: Secondary graphic elements: some visuals or icons may have been optimized or generated using AI tools.
about_ia_visual_conclusion: Rather than smoothing over this diversity, I embrace it. It reflects the reality of today’s creators: a constant intertwining of artisanal and technological tools.
about_ia_megaprompt_text: For certain complex sections of the documentation, I used long and structured prompts (often more than 500 words). In accordance with my own arbitration rule (section "/pages/documentation.html#dc-4.2.6"4.2.6 of the documentation), I considered these passages as HCE.
about_ia_megaprompt_reason: Why? Because the effort of structuring the prompt was such that the AI had no margin for inference or invention; it was merely executing a human “conceptual dictation.”
about_ia_goodfaith_intro: TCP/UP is a citizen initiative. It is neither an official declaration nor an infallible technical proof system.
about_ia_goodfaith_text: Its effectiveness rests on a single pillar: the author’s sincerity. Applying this protocol to my own approach is not a marketing exercise; it is a choice of coherence. I do not use AI to replace myself, but to enhance my ability to formalize and test my ideas.
about_ia_labels_intro: In accordance with the charter, I have applied the following signatures in the site’s metadata and footer:
about_ia_labels_conclusion: “Humans define the intention, assume the arbitrations, and bear responsibility for the content. AI intervenes as an assistance or co-production tool depending on the case, in an explicitly declared manner.”
about_blog_heading: The blog: an editorial laboratory for TCP/UP
about_blog_intro: The TCP/UP protocol is not meant to remain a fixed theoretical framework. I therefore wanted to give it a living space, where it can be confronted with concrete cases, regulatory news, press articles, professional uses, and real production situations.
about_blog_desc: "../blog/"The blog fulfills this role. I publish regular monitoring on AI transparency, European regulation, debates around labeling, traceability, and editorial responsibility. I also offer article analyses, critical readings, and practical applications of TCP/UP to test the robustness of the protocol against real-world conditions.
about_blog_explanation: This blog is therefore not a simple opinion space. It is a place of documentation, experimentation, and clarification, designed to gradually bring forth a usable, readable, and debatable doctrine of transparency. "../blog/"Access the blog
about_blog_quote: “If you are discovering TCP/UP, the blog is often the best entry point to understand how the protocol applies to concrete situations.”
about_gouvernance_now_text: TCP/UP was born from a personal conviction. At this stage, I am the sole decision-maker regarding the evolution of the protocol. This situation would not be ideal if the project were to scale and be widely adopted.
about_gouvernance_future_text: My goal is to build this gradually. For now, this governance is still under construction. Discussion and contribution channels are being set up. Interested individuals can already contact me to express their interest.
about_gouvernance_participate_item1: Join the discussion channel on "https://bsky.app/profile/tcp-up.org" Bluesky and "https://www.linkedin.com/company/tcp-up" LinkedIn
about_gouvernance_participate_item2: "/pages/contact.html"Contact me if you wish to participate
about_gouvernance_participate_item3: Consult the "https://github.com/taltan/tcp-up" GitHub repository to follow the evolution of the protocol and propose improvements
